summary: Contract-first API design
context: Define API contracts before implementation to enable parallel FE/BE work
impact: Eliminates integration blockers and rework
applicability: All projects with separate frontend and backend teams

summary: Parallel work with mocks
context: Frontend uses mock API responses while backend builds real endpoints
impact: Reduces delivery time by enabling concurrent development
applicability: Any project where FE and BE can be decoupled via contracts

summary: Early QA involvement
context: QA reviews specs and writes test plans during Discovery, not after implementation
impact: Catches spec gaps before they become code defects
applicability: All projects

summary: Clear separation of concerns
context: Each agent owns distinct files and domains; no cross-writing
impact: Prevents merge conflicts and overwrite bugs in parallel work
applicability: All agent team projects

summary: Compute-on-demand (never store predictions)
context: Prediction results are never persisted. The rules engine recomputes trajectories on every request from stored lab entries or inline guest data. This was a deliberate architectural decision (Decision #10) to minimize the number of PHI tables and avoid cache invalidation complexity.
impact: Reduces HIPAA surface area, eliminates a predictions table, simplifies the data model, and avoids stale-prediction bugs. Phase 3 can add storage if FDA audit trail demands it.
applicability: Any health/compliance project where computed results are derived from stored inputs and regulatory surface area should be minimized

summary: Two-endpoint pattern: separate storage from computation
context: POST /lab-entries stores data; POST /predict computes trajectories. Authenticated users reference stored entry IDs; guests send inline data. Storage and computation are independent operations with different auth requirements and HIPAA implications (Decision #8).
impact: Clean separation of concerns between persistent resources and stateless computation. Allows guests to use the tool without accounts while keeping the storage path auth-gated.
applicability: Any application where data persistence and derived computation serve different user segments or have different security profiles

summary: Magic link auth (passwordless)
context: No passwords stored anywhere. Authentication uses single-use magic link tokens (SHA-256 hashed, 15-min TTL) delivered via email. JWT access tokens (1hr) in memory, refresh tokens (7d) with rotation. Eliminates credential stuffing, bcrypt overhead, password reset flows, and password storage liability (Decision #1).
impact: Dramatically reduces auth attack surface and HIPAA credential exposure. Simpler UX for elderly patients who frequently forget passwords.
applicability: Any application targeting non-technical users where email is a reliable channel and password management is a friction point

summary: Guest-to-user data promotion flow
context: Guests get an httpOnly session cookie on first prediction. Lab data is stored server-side in JSONB with 24hr TTL. On magic link verify, guest session data atomically migrates to proper lab_entries rows, the guest session is deleted, and the cookie is cleared. The verify response includes a guest_data_migrated flag.
impact: Zero-friction onboarding: users try the product before committing. No data loss on account creation. Clean transition from ephemeral to permanent storage.
applicability: Any product with a try-before-you-sign-up pattern where guest data needs to survive account creation

summary: Confidence tier system for progressive data quality
context: Three tiers of prediction confidence based on data completeness: Tier 1 = required fields only; Tier 2 = required + BOTH hemoglobin AND glucose (not either alone, Decision #12); Tier 3 = Tier 2 + 3 or more visit dates with slope analysis. Each tier shows a badge, an unlock prompt for the next tier, and progressively richer prediction output.
impact: Encourages users to provide more data without making it mandatory. Provides honest confidence signaling. Gamifies data entry through progressive unlocks.
applicability: Any prediction or analysis tool where input completeness affects output quality and users should be nudged toward better inputs

summary: HIPAA-compliant guest session handling with TTL purge
context: Guest data stored in guest_sessions table as JSONB with 24hr TTL (expires_at DEFAULT NOW() + 24hr). A pg_cron job runs every 15 minutes to DELETE expired rows. Each purge run is audit-logged. Guest data is classified as PHI with full encryption and audit protections. Alert if purge hasn't run in 30 minutes (2x interval).
impact: Balances usability (patients can return within 24hr) with PHI minimization. Stale data never lingers. Purge monitoring prevents silent compliance drift.
applicability: Any HIPAA/PII system with ephemeral user sessions where data retention must be time-bounded

summary: Standardized error envelope across all endpoints
context: Every 4xx/5xx response uses {error: {code, message, details[{field, message}]}}. Machine-readable code for programmatic handling, human-readable message safe to display, and field-level details array for inline form validation. Consistent across all 12 endpoints (Decision #9).
impact: Frontend can implement a single error handler for all API calls. Field-level errors map directly to form inputs. Contract tests validate the envelope shape on every error code.
applicability: All API projects. This pattern should be a default for any new backend.

summary: Contract-first OpenAPI spec driving parallel development
context: John publishes the OpenAPI 3.1 spec to /artifacts/api_contract.json. This spec is the single source of truth: Harshit builds MSW mocks from it, Yuri runs Schemathesis contract tests against it, Gay Mark aligns DB column names to it, and John implements FastAPI against it. Example responses in the spec become mock data. Any change requires team discussion and CTO approval.
impact: All four workstreams (FE, BE, DB, QA) develop in parallel against the same contract. Integration happens last with high confidence. MSW mocks stay aligned via contract tests.
applicability: Any project with 3+ parallel workstreams that share an API boundary

summary: Visx chart rendering with accessible SVG
context: Visx (D3 primitives as React components) renders eGFR trajectories as SVG. Full control over dash patterns, phase bands, label collision avoidance (15px min separation), and responsive sizing via @visx/responsive ParentSize. A hidden AccessibleDataTable provides screen-reader alternative. ARIA labels on all SVG elements. Two chart variants (full interactive vs. simplified fallback) gated on Sprint 0 POC.
impact: SVG output gives pixel-level control for accessibility attributes, custom line styles, and visual regression testing. No opinionated chart framework limits customization.
applicability: Any medical/scientific visualization where accessibility, custom styling, and deterministic rendering are critical

summary: Test pyramid ratios (60/30/10) with strict coverage thresholds
context: 60% unit tests (fast feedback, boundary-value testing on 15+ input fields), 30% integration tests (API contract via Schemathesis, DB round-trips, auth flows), 10% E2E tests (8 critical journeys in Playwright). Coverage thresholds: backend 90% line, frontend 85% line, 100% contract endpoint coverage, 0 critical axe-core violations. All PR-blocking.
impact: Fast CI feedback loop (unit tests < 5s). Expensive E2E tests kept minimal but cover every release-blocking path. No deployment without all gates passing.
applicability: All projects. Adjust the ratio only with QA justification.

summary: MSW for frontend mocking during parallel development
context: Mock Service Worker v2 intercepts API calls in development and testing, returning responses matching the published OpenAPI contract. Mock fixtures organized by tier (prediction-tier1.json, prediction-tier2.json, prediction-tier3.json) plus error states. Contract tests validate that MSW mocks stay aligned with the real API. Integration with real endpoints happens last by swapping handlers.
impact: Frontend development is never blocked by backend progress. Mock data accuracy improves once real test vectors arrive. Contract test alignment prevents mock drift.
applicability: Any project with parallel FE/BE development where the API contract is published before implementation

summary: Executive-first PRD format (scannable body + deep appendices)
context: The PRD has a scannable executive summary and 5 main sections (summary, milestones, roles, technical considerations, open questions) followed by 6 deep appendices — one per domain agent (API, DB, Frontend, UX, Test, Architecture). Each appendix is the agent's finalized V2 domain document included verbatim. Stakeholders read the body; implementers reference their appendix.
impact: Two reading paths: 5-minute executive scan and deep-dive reference. Each agent's authoritative spec is preserved intact. No information loss in synthesis.
applicability: Any multi-domain project where the PRD must serve both executive stakeholders and technical implementers

summary: Two-meeting discovery process with V1-debate-V2-synthesis flow
context: Discovery follows a strict 6-step sequence: (1) Memory bootstrap, (2) Independent V1 domain drafts, (3) Meeting 1 (present V1, debate, decide), (4) Independent V2 revisions incorporating feedback, (5) Meeting 2 (cross-review V2, resolve outliers), (6) PM synthesizes final PRD after Meeting 2. Critical rule: PRD is a post-Meeting-2 synthesis, never a parallel draft.
impact: V1 drafts force each agent to form an independent opinion before group influence. Meeting 1 debate surfaces conflicts early. V2 revisions incorporate cross-disciplinary feedback. Meeting 2 catches remaining misalignments. PRD synthesis ensures all finalized positions are included.
applicability: All agent team discovery phases. The sequence is load-bearing — skipping steps produces lower-quality specs.

summary: Defense-in-depth validation (API + DB CHECK constraints)
context: All lab value ranges are validated at both layers: Pydantic models in FastAPI (first line) and CHECK constraints in PostgreSQL (last line). If a bug in the API layer allows invalid data through, the DB rejects it with a constraint violation. CHECK ranges match API validation ranges exactly. When John updates a range in the API contract, Gay Mark updates the corresponding CHECK.
impact: Prevents corrupt clinical data from ever being stored, even if the application layer has bugs. Two layers of defense for data integrity.
applicability: Any application storing validated data where data corruption has serious consequences (health, financial, legal)

summary: Audit log with ON DELETE SET NULL for compliance survival
context: audit_log.user_id uses ON DELETE SET NULL (Decision #14). When a patient deletes their account, all lab_entries and magic_link_tokens cascade-delete, but audit records are preserved with user_id = NULL. The deletion event itself is logged before the CASCADE executes. HIPAA requires retaining the audit trail for a minimum of 6 years. app_service role has INSERT-only access to audit_log — no UPDATE or DELETE.
impact: Satisfies HIPAA right-to-delete while preserving the compliance audit trail. Audit integrity is maintained even if the application is compromised.
applicability: Any HIPAA/SOX/GDPR-compliant system with user deletion requirements and mandatory audit trails

summary: UUID primary keys with BIGSERIAL exception for write-heavy tables
context: All application tables (users, lab_entries, guest_sessions, magic_link_tokens) use UUID v4 via gen_random_uuid() to prevent sequential ID enumeration attacks (OWASP A01:2021). The audit_log table is the exception — BIGSERIAL for write performance since it is append-heavy and never queried by PK via API.
impact: Prevents ID enumeration attacks on patient data. Safe for distributed generation. Write-heavy audit table gets sequential insert performance.
applicability: Any security-sensitive application. Use UUIDs by default; BIGSERIAL only for internal append-only tables not exposed via API.

summary: Two-role RBAC (app_service + app_admin)
context: Two database roles enforce least privilege: app_service (DML on data tables, INSERT-only on audit_log) for the FastAPI runtime, and app_admin (ALL privileges) for Alembic migrations and DDL. app_service cannot UPDATE or DELETE audit_log rows. No shared credentials between environments. No direct DB access from frontend.
impact: Limits blast radius of application compromise. Audit trail integrity is enforced at the database level. Migration privileges are isolated from runtime.
applicability: All production database deployments, especially those handling regulated data

summary: Progressive disclosure form layout (required-optional-silent sections)
context: The prediction form uses a single-screen progressive disclosure pattern: required fields shown first, optional fields in a collapsible 'Sharpen your prediction' section, and silent fields in a collapsible 'Additional health info' section. Not a multi-step wizard. Users see all available inputs on one page but only the required fields demand attention.
impact: Reduces cognitive load for the 60+ target audience. Required fields get attention first. Optional fields are discoverable without being overwhelming. Single-page avoids navigation confusion.
applicability: Any form with a mix of required and optional fields, especially for non-technical users or elderly audiences

summary: Responsive disclaimer strategy (inline desktop, sticky footer mobile)
context: Three verbatim legal disclaimers display inline below chart/cards on desktop where space allows. On mobile, they appear as a sticky footer collapsed by default with an expand-on-tap affordance (Decision #11). This keeps disclaimers accessible without burying the chart. Legal confirmation required that collapsed display satisfies requirements.
impact: Legal compliance without destroying the mobile UX. Disclaimers are always reachable but don't dominate the viewport on small screens.
applicability: Any regulated application (health, finance) with mandatory legal text that must be visible across form factors

summary: Five QA gates per sprint (Spec-Contract-Component-Integration-PreRelease)
context: Every sprint has 5 blocking gates: Gate 0 (Spec Review — testable AC), Gate 1 (Contract Lock — Schemathesis passes), Gate 2 (Component Review — unit tests + axe-core + visual baselines), Gate 3 (Integration — FE-BE round-trips + E2E), Gate 4 (Pre-Release — full regression + HIPAA checklist + manual exploratory). Each gate requires explicit QA approval to advance.
impact: Defects are caught at the earliest possible stage. No story moves to Done without QA sign-off. No deployment without full regression.
applicability: All projects with sprint-based delivery and regulatory or quality requirements. Reduce gate count only with explicit QA approval.

summary: Schemathesis for automated API contract testing
context: Schemathesis auto-generates tests from the OpenAPI 3.1 spec, validating every endpoint/method/status combination including boundary values, missing fields, and malformed requests. It runs against both mock server (Gate 1) and live staging API (Gate 3). Preferred over Dredd for OpenAPI 3.1 support. Contract tests run before integration tests — if the contract is broken, nothing downstream matters.
impact: Catches API contract violations automatically without hand-writing every test case. Ensures 100% endpoint coverage. Prevents frontend-backend contract drift.
applicability: Any project with an OpenAPI spec. Should be a default tool for API contract validation.

summary: Synthetic deterministic seed data for testing
context: No real patient data in any environment (HIPAA requirement). Seed data uses fixed UUIDs and deterministic values covering all tiers, boundary values, and edge cases. Five seed users (Tier 1, Tier 2, Tier 3, sex=unknown, delete-me) with corresponding lab entries, guest sessions (active, expired, about-to-expire), and pre-populated audit log records. Seed script is test infrastructure only, never in the Alembic migration chain.
impact: Tests produce repeatable results. All edge cases are pre-covered. No compliance risk from test data. Migration chain stays clean.
applicability: All HIPAA/PII projects. Useful even for non-regulated projects where test determinism matters.

summary: Server components by default, client components only for interactivity
context: Next.js 15 App Router uses server components for layouts, static content, metadata, and SEO. Client components ('use client') only where interactivity requires it: PredictionForm, PredictionChart, auth flows, disclaimer expand/collapse. No 'use client' on layout components unless absolutely necessary. Static shell rendered at the edge; interactive elements hydrated on client.
impact: Smaller JS bundle sizes, faster initial page loads, better SEO for static content. Clear boundary between server-rendered and client-interactive code.
applicability: All Next.js 13+ App Router projects. Default to server; opt into client only when needed.

summary: Monolithic backend with embedded rules engine for MVP
context: The proprietary prediction rules engine runs as an embedded Python module within FastAPI, not a separate microservice. Coefficients and logic are never exposed to the client or logged. Phase 3 consideration: extract to a separate internal service (gRPC/HTTP) if scaling or FDA audit requirements demand it.
impact: Simplifies deployment (single container), reduces latency (no network hop), and keeps the IP isolated. Avoids premature microservice complexity.
applicability: Any MVP where a single backend service is sufficient. Extract to microservice only when scale, audit, or multi-consumer needs demand it.

summary: Anti-enumeration responses (identical response for existing/nonexistent accounts)
context: POST /auth/request-link returns an identical 200 response whether the email exists or not: 'If an account exists for this email, a magic link has been sent.' This prevents email enumeration attacks. New accounts are auto-created on first magic link request.
impact: Attackers cannot determine which emails have accounts. Reduces social engineering surface. Simplifies the auth flow (no separate registration endpoint).
applicability: Any application with email-based auth where user privacy matters. Should be a default for all auth endpoints.

summary: Dual decision authority (CTO for technical, PM for product)
context: In discovery debates, the CTO has final vote on technical disputes (frameworks, tools, architecture, infrastructure) and the PM has final vote on product/user disputes (scope, priority, UX, acceptance criteria). When technical and product concerns conflict, CTO defers to PM on user impact, PM defers to CTO on technical feasibility. Deadlocks are resolved by mutual deference.
impact: Prevents analysis paralysis in debates. Clear authority prevents design-by-committee. Mutual deference clause handles the edge case of cross-domain conflicts.
applicability: All agent team projects with both technical and product leadership

summary: Accessibility-first design for elderly users (44px targets, 16px fonts, WCAG AA)
context: Target audience is 60+ CKD patients with reduced fine motor control and potential visual impairment. 44px minimum touch targets (WCAG 2.5.5 AAA), 16px minimum input font size (prevents iOS auto-zoom), 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text, hidden data table alternative for screen reader users, keyboard navigation maps for all flows, Inter font for excellent number rendering.
impact: Usable by the actual patient population, not just young testers. Prevents iOS zoom bug. Screen reader alternative ensures chart data is accessible.
applicability: Any health application targeting elderly or mobility-impaired users. These minimums should be considered for all consumer health apps.
